paper.title: The Chemical Composition of Extreme-Velocity Stars

review.summary: This paper presents high-resolution spectroscopic observations of 15 extreme-velocity star candidates. Internal data assets include: (1) an observation log (Table~\\ref{obs_log}) recording coordinates, dates, exposure times, S/N, and instruments; (2) a line-by-line atomic data, equivalent width, and abundance table (Table~\\ref{measured_ews}) with a full machine-readable version available; (3) a comprehensive stellar parameters and orbital properties table (Table~\\ref{stellar_params}) combining photometry, derived atmospheric parameters (Teff, log g, [Fe/H], xi), and Galactic orbit integrations (RV, v, Rperi, Rapo, e, zmax, Etot, Lz); and (4) a chemical abundances table (Table~\\ref{chem_abundances_tbl}) reporting mean abundances and uncertainties for 20+ elements across all stars. External resources include spectroscopic reduction/analysis software (CarPy, iSpec, q2, MOOG, isochrones, MultiNest, galpy), atomic line data repositories (linemake, Kurucz linelists, INSPECT project), and astrometric/photometric surveys (Gaia EDR3, 2MASS, WISE, SkyMapper).

internal_tables.role_in_paper: Records the observational details for each of the 15 target stars, including Gaia EDR3 source identifiers, celestial coordinates, observation dates/times, spectrograph slit widths, exposure times, signal-to-noise ratios at two wavelengths, and the instruments used (ARCES or MIKE).

columns.name: Source ID | EDR3
columns.meaning: Gaia EDR3 source identifier for the target star

columns.name: R.A. | (h:m:s)
columns.meaning: Right Ascension in hours:minutes:seconds

columns.name: Decl. | (d:m:s)
columns.meaning: Declination in degrees:arcminutes:arcseconds

columns.name: UT Date
columns.meaning: Universal Time date of the observation

columns.name: Start
columns.meaning: Local start time of the observation

columns.name: Slit | Width
columns.meaning: Spectrograph slit width

columns.name: Exposure | Time (s)
columns.meaning: Total exposure time of the observation

columns.name: S/N | \\colhead4500 \\rm\\AA
columns.meaning: Signal-to-noise ratio at 4500 Angstroms

columns.name: S/N | \\colhead6500 \\rm\\AA
columns.meaning: Signal-to-noise ratio at 6500 Angstroms

columns.name: Instrument
columns.meaning: Spectrograph used for the observation (ARCES or MIKE)

internal_tables.role_in_paper: Contains the input atomic line data, measured equivalent widths, and line-by-line absolute abundances for each spectral line used in the chemical analysis. The full version is published in machine-readable format.

source_refs.caption: Atomic data, Equivalent Widths and line Abundances. Full version online.\\label{measured_ews}
source_refs.label: measured_ews

columns.name: Gaia EDR3 Source ID
columns.meaning: Gaia EDR3 source identifier for the target star

columns.name: Wavelength | (\\AA)
columns.meaning: Rest wavelength of the atomic absorption line

columns.name: Species
columns.meaning: Ion species of the atomic line (e.g., Na I, Mg I, Fe II)

columns.name: Excitation Potential | (eV)
columns.meaning: Excitation potential of the lower energy level of the transition

columns.name: log(gf)
columns.meaning: Logarithm of the product of the statistical weight and oscillator strength of the transition

columns.name: EW | (m\\AA)
columns.meaning: Measured equivalent width of the absorption line

columns.name: \\colhead\\log_\\epsilon(\\rmX)
columns.meaning: Absolute abundance of element X in the log-epsilon scale derived from the individual line

internal_tables.evidence: Atomic data, Equivalent Widths and line Abundances. Full version online.\\label{measured_ews}

internal_tables.role_in_paper: Presents photometric properties, adopted stellar atmospheric parameters (effective temperature, surface gravity, metallicity, microturbulence), and orbital parameters (radial velocity, total Galactic velocity, pericenter, apocenter, eccentricity, maximum distance from plane, total energy, angular momentum) for all 15 program stars.

columns.name: Property
columns.meaning: Name of the physical or observational property (e.g., photometric band, stellar parameter, or orbital parameter)

columns.meaning: Units of the property listed in the corresponding row

internal_tables.role_in_paper: Reports the adopted mean chemical abundances and associated uncertainties for each measured element (alpha, light odd-Z, iron-peak, and neutron-capture elements) across all 15 program stars. Each star occupies four columns: number of lines, mean log-epsilon abundance, mean [X/Fe] ratio, and standard deviation.

columns.meaning: Mean abundance ratio of element X relative to iron

columns.meaning: Standard deviation or uncertainty of the [X/Fe] abundance ratio

external_resources.description: CarPy software package for reducing raw MIKE spectrograph data and calibration frames.

source_refs.context:
lamp frames) in the afternoon before each night of observations.
We reduced the raw spectra and calibration frames using the
\texttt{CarPy}\footnote{\url{http://code.obs.carnegiescience.edu/mike}}
software package \citep{kelson2000,kelson2003,kelson2014}. On ARCES, we used 
the standard 1\farcs6 slit yielding effective spectra with continuous coverage

external_resources.evidence: Used to reduce raw spectra and calibration frames from the MIKE spectrograph.

external_resources.description: iSpec software used for calculating radial velocities, barycentric corrections, and spectral normalization.

source_refs.context:
CERES pipeline \citep{brahm2017}. 
We present a log of these observations in Table~\ref{obs_log}.
We used \texttt{iSpec}\footnote{\url{https://www.blancocuaresma.com/s/iSpec}}
\citep{blanco-cuaresma2014,blanco-cuaresma2019} to calculate radial
velocities and barycentric corrections and to normalize the spectra. Our

external_resources.evidence: Used to calculate radial velocities and barycentric corrections and to normalize the spectra.

external_resources.description: linemake code providing atomic and molecular line data for spectroscopic abundance analysis.
external_resources.evidence: Source of atomic and molecular data for selected spectral lines used in abundance analysis.

external_resources.description: q2 MOOG wrapper used for deriving stellar metallicities from spectroscopic data.
external_resources.evidence: Used with ATLAS9 model atmospheres and MOOG radiative transfer code to derive stellar metallicities.

external_resources.description: isochrones Python package used to self-consistently infer atmospheric and fundamental stellar parameters by fitting isochrones to photometric and astrometric data.
external_resources.evidence: Used to self-consistently infer atmospheric and fundamental stellar parameters for each star.

external_resources.description: MultiNest nested sampling algorithm used for Bayesian parameter estimation when fitting stellar isochrones.
external_resources.evidence: Used through PyMultinest to fit the MIST isochrone library to observational data.

external_resources.description: galpy Python library for Galactic dynamics used to calculate the Galactic orbits of the sample stars.

source_refs.context:
We calculated the Galactic orbits of our sample stars using
\texttt{galpy}\footnote{\url{https://github.com/jobovy/galpy}}. 
\texttt{Galpy} uses a left-handed coordinate system, with the Sun at positive X 
and positive L$_{\mathrm{Z}}$ indicates a prograde orbit.

external_resources.evidence: Used to calculate the Galactic orbits of the sample stars.

external_resources.description: INSPECT project website providing non-LTE abundance correction grids, used for sodium abundance corrections.

source_refs.context:

strongly affected \citep{zhao2016}. We corrected sodium abundances using the \citet{lind2011}
grid through the INSPECT project\footnote{http://inspect-stars.com/}. 
The aluminum non-LE abundance corrections are from \cite{nordlander2017}. 
We obtained potassium abundance corrections via a linear interpolation

external_resources.evidence: Used to apply non-LTE corrections to sodium abundances via the Lind et al. 2011 grid.

external_resources.description: Kurucz atomic line lists providing hyperfine structure data used for yttrium abundance estimation.

source_refs.context:
Yttrium abundances were estimated 
using hyperfine structure data from the 
Kurucz\footnote{http://kurucz.harvard.edu/linelists.html} linelists. Barium abundances 
were calculated using the isotopic splitting from \cite{mcwilliam1998} and \cite{klose2002}. 
Europium abundances were estimated using spectral synthesis from

external_resources.evidence: Source of hyperfine structure data used for yttrium abundance estimation.
